text:
CT CHEST, ABDOMEN, AND PELVIS WITH IV CONTRAST
Post-Treatment Response Assessment

CLINICAL INDICATION:
Marcus T. Delgado, 48-year-old male with recently diagnosed de novo acute myeloid leukemia (AML), FAB M1, FLT3-ITD high allelic ratio, ELN adverse risk. Patient completed induction chemotherapy approximately 30 days prior (7+3 regimen: cytarabine continuous infusion days 1-7, daunorubicin days 1-3, with midostaurin days 8-21). Presenting imaging had demonstrated hepatosplenomegaly consistent with leukemic infiltration. Current study requested for post-induction response assessment and evaluation of extramedullary disease.

TECHNIQUE:
Multidetector CT acquisition of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis performed in the portal venous phase following administration of 100 mL of nonionic IV contrast (iohexol 350). Oral contrast was not administered. Images reconstructed at 2.5 mm axial slice thickness with coronal and sagittal multiplanar reformats. Dose modulation employed. Comparison made with prior CT from approximately 30 days ago (pre-treatment staging).

FINDINGS:

CHEST:

Lungs and Pleura: The lung parenchyma is clear throughout. No pulmonary nodules identified. No areas of consolidation, ground-glass opacity, or air space disease. The airways are patent to the segmental level bilaterally. No pleural effusions. No pneumothorax.

Mediastinum: The mediastinal contour is unremarkable. No mediastinal, hilar, or paratracheal lymph node enlargement. The great vessels appear normal in caliber with appropriate contrast opacification. The esophagus is not dilated. No pericardial effusion. The cardiac silhouette is within normal limits. PICC line tip is noted in the right atrium/SVC junction, unremarkable in appearance.

Chest Wall and Axillae: No axillary lymphadenopathy. No chest wall masses or soft tissue abnormalities.

Bones of the Thorax: The visualized ribs, sternum, thoracic vertebrae, and bilateral clavicles and scapulae demonstrate no focal lytic or sclerotic lesions. The marrow attenuation of the thoracic vertebrae appears homogeneous without the diffuse low-attenuation signal abnormality previously suggesting marrow infiltration — this is a qualitative change from the pre-treatment examination and is consistent with marrow clearance following chemotherapy-induced aplasia and early reconstitution.

ABDOMEN:

Liver: The liver now measures 14.8 cm in craniocaudal span on coronal reconstruction, representing a substantial decrease from the prior measurement of 17.2 cm. The hepatic parenchyma enhances homogeneously without focal lesions, cysts, or masses. The hepatic veins are patent. The portal vein is patent with normal caliber. The intrahepatic biliary ducts are not dilated. The gallbladder is present and unremarkable, with no cholelithiasis identified. The common bile duct measures 4 mm in diameter, within normal limits. No perihepatic fluid or ascites.

Spleen: The spleen has returned to a normal configuration and measures 9.4 cm in longest axis on axial imaging, markedly decreased from the prior measurement of 14.1 cm. The splenic parenchyma is homogeneous with appropriate contrast enhancement. No splenic lesions, infarcts, or accessory spleen. No perisplenic fluid.

Pancreas: The pancreas is normal in size, morphology, and enhancement. No pancreatic ductal dilatation. No peripancreatic stranding.

Adrenal Glands: Both adrenal glands are normal in morphology. No adrenal nodules or masses.

Kidneys and Ureters: Both kidneys demonstrate symmetric and homogeneous enhancement. No hydronephrosis. No nephrolithiasis. No renal masses or cysts. Bilateral kidneys are normal in size. No perinephric stranding.

Lymph Nodes: No retroperitoneal, aortocaval, mesenteric, or celiac axis lymph node enlargement. Multiple small sub-centimeter lymph nodes are present in the mesentery, none of which are pathologically enlarged by size criteria and are likely reactive in etiology given recent systemic illness. No discrete lymph node masses.

Bowel: The stomach appears normal with appropriate gastric wall thickness. Small bowel loops appear normal in caliber, without wall thickening or abnormal enhancement. The colon appears normal throughout without wall thickening, masses, or obstruction. The appendix is identified and is normal in appearance.

Peritoneum and Vasculature: No ascites. No peritoneal soft tissue implants or nodularity. The aorta is normal in caliber throughout the abdomen. The celiac axis, superior mesenteric artery, and inferior mesenteric artery appear patent.

PELVIS:

Lymph Nodes: No pelvic, iliac, or inguinal lymph node enlargement.

Bladder: The urinary bladder is partially distended and the wall appears unremarkable without focal thickening or masses.

Pelvis/Reproductive Structures: The prostate gland is normal in size without nodularity. No pelvic free fluid.

Bones of the Abdomen and Pelvis: The visualized lumbar vertebrae, sacrum, bilateral iliac wings, and proximal femora demonstrate no focal lytic, sclerotic, or permeative osseous lesions. Importantly, the diffuse marrow signal changes noted throughout the axial skeleton on the prior examination — which had been interpreted as reflecting marrow infiltration by leukemia — are no longer apparent. The marrow of the visualized osseous structures is now relatively homogeneous in attenuation, compatible with a treated/recovering marrow without evidence of ongoing gross leukemic replacement. No cortical destruction. No periosteal reaction. No soft tissue extension from osseous structures to suggest extramedullary granulocytic sarcoma (chloroma) formation.

SOFT TISSUES: No soft tissue masses or collections. No subcutaneous abnormality.

IMPRESSION:

1. Marked improvement compared with pre-treatment staging CT. The previously noted hepatomegaly has resolved, with the liver now measuring within normal limits at 14.8 cm craniocaudal span (previously 17.2 cm).

2. The previously documented splenomegaly has similarly resolved. The spleen now measures 9.4 cm (previously 14.1 cm), within the normal range.

3. No evidence of extramedullary AML. Specifically, no chloroma (granulocytic sarcoma) is identified in any soft tissue, orbital, epidural, or paraspinal location. No abnormal soft tissue masses in the mediastinum, abdomen, or pelvis to suggest an extramedullary leukemic deposit.

4. No lymphadenopathy in the chest, abdomen, or pelvis.

5. The diffuse osseous marrow changes previously suspicious for leukemic marrow infiltration are no longer apparent, consistent with post-chemotherapy marrow response.

6. No new pulmonary infiltrates or consolidation to suggest an infectious complication.

7. PICC catheter tip in expected position at the SVC/right atrial junction.

Overall, the imaging findings are in keeping with a favorable response to induction chemotherapy, with resolution of the organomegaly that was present at initial presentation. No imaging evidence of residual extramedullary disease.

Attending Radiologist: [Electronically signed]
Residency Trainee: [Electronically signed]
